chunks.title: CSCE 624 Syllabus
chunks.content: Sketch Recognition. (3-0). Credit 3. Analysis, implementation, and comparison of sketch recognition algorithms, including feature-based, vision-based, geometrical, timing-based, and path-based recognition algorithms; methods for combining these recognition methods for greater accuracy, using known AI techniques. Prerequisite: Graduate classification.

chunks.title: Additional Course Details
chunks.content:
Sketch recognition sits at the intersection of artificial intelligence, human-computer interaction, and visual communication. This course emphasizes how humans naturally sketch, how systems interpret sketches, and how recognition algorithms behave in real-world, noisy settings.

The course is structured around:
• hands-on implementation of recognition techniques,
• critical reading of classic and contemporary research papers,
• in-class activities that build intuition for algorithms and features,
• and a semester-long team project that mirrors a research or advanced development workflow.

The course is discussion-driven and interactive. Students are expected to actively engage with readings, participate in in-class activities, and reflect on tradeoffs between algorithmic approaches rather than focusing on a single “correct" solution.

chunks.title: Course Prerequisites
chunks.content:
Prerequisite/Corequisite(s): Graduate classification.

Students are expected to have general graduate-level knowledge in computer science. Familiarity with at least one of the following will be helpful but is not strictly required:
• programming (any language),
• basic machine learning or artificial intelligence concepts,
• linear algebra or probability,
• or software engineering practices.

Students from related disciplines (e.g., engineering, HCI, data science) are welcome and have historically done well in the course.

chunks.title: Course Learning Outcomes
chunks.content:
Upon completion of this course, the learner will be able to:
• Explain core principles of sketch recognition, including data capture, preprocessing, and feature generation.
• Implement and compare multiple sketch recognition approaches, including feature-based, vision-based, geometric, temporal, and path-based methods.
• Apply and evaluate machine learning classifiers in the context of sketch data.
• Analyze algorithm performance using appropriate statistical and experimental evaluation techniques.
• Design and conduct sketch-based experiments that account for human variability, interface design, and data collection constraints.
• Critically read, interpret, and discuss research literature in sketch recognition and related AI/HCI domains.
• Collaborate effectively on a team-based project that integrates algorithmic design, implementation, and evaluation.

chunks.title: Textbook and/or Resource Materials
chunks.content:
This material Is: Required
No official textbook - material provided by instructor
Notes:
Assigned readings will consist of:
• peer-reviewed research papers,
• selected book chapters,
• and instructor-provided notes.

The course will make use of:
• Canvas for announcements, readings, and grades,
• Gradescope for assignment submission and feedback,
• instructor-provided datasets, code examples, and worksheets,
• and AI-based tools where explicitly permitted and documented.

chunks.title: Grading Policy
chunks.content:
Graded Components and Weights
Final grades are based on accumulated points across the semester.
• Assignments: 30%
• Project (team-based): 30%
• Assessments (quizzes and exams): 30%
• In-class activities and participation: 10%
Total: 100%

chunks.title: Grade Scale
chunks.content:
• A: 90-100
• B: 80-<90
• C: 70-<80
• D: 60-<70
• F: < 60

This course is taught to mastery. Grades are not curved.

chunks.title: Late Work Policy
chunks.content: Work submitted by a student as makeup work for an excused absence is not considered late work and is exempt from the late work policy (Student Rule 7).

chunks.title: Course Specific Late Work Policy
chunks.content:
Assignments submitted after the deadline will be accepted with a penalty of 20% per day late, unless prior arrangements have been made or the absence qualifies as excused under university policy.

Late work beyond several days may not receive feedback.

chunks.title: Course Specific Attendance Policy
chunks.content:
This course relies heavily on in-class discussion and activities.

Students are expected to attend class regularly and participate actively. If attendance or participation is graded, expectations and evaluation criteria will be clearly communicated in advance.

Students who must miss class for an excused reason should notify the instructor as early as possible.

chunks.title: Course Specific Makeup Work Policy
chunks.content:
Make-up work for excused absences will be handled on a case-by-case basis in accordance with University Student Rule 7.

Students requesting make-up work must communicate promptly and provide required documentation. The instructor is not obligated to provide make-up opportunities for unexcused absences.

chunks.title: Course Schedule
chunks.content:
The course follows a traditional 15-week semester schedule.

Weeks 1-3
Foundations of Sketch Recognition
• Sketch data, representation, and preprocessing
• Feature generation and human sketch behavior

Weeks 4-5
Data Handling and Evaluation
• Data handling and transformation
• Recognition models and evaluation
• Classifiers, decision strategies, and tradeoffs
• Performance metrics and error analysis

Midterm 1: Thursday, February 12

Weeks 6-7
Core Recognition Approaches
• Geometric, temporal, path-based, and hybrid approaches
• Statistical reasoning and interpretation

Weeks 8-9
Mathematical and Sequential Models
• Linear algebra concepts for recognition
• Hidden Markov Models, LLMs, and related techniques

Midterm 2: Thursday, March 26

Weeks 10-12
Advanced Models and Applications
• Advanced and sequential models
• Modern and emerging approaches
• Applications and case studies

Weeks 13-15
Final Project Integration
• Final project presentations
• Synthesis and analysis

The schedule reflects the intended flow of topics; specific ordering and pacing may be adjusted to best support student learning.

chunks.title: AI Statement
chunks.content:
AI tools may be used when explicitly permitted for assignments or projects. Any AI assistance must be clearly documented, including:
• what tool was used,
• how it was used,
• and how the student validated or modified the output.

Students remain fully responsible for the correctness, originality, and understanding of all submitted work.
